text: We present the case of an incidentally found cystic lesion of the pancreatic tail in a completely asymptomatic young Caucasian female who was submitted to abdominal pelvic ultrasonography. After CT scan, MRI posed the hypothesis of solid pseudopapillary tumor of the pancreas due to hyperintensity in TR sequences and to slight restriction of the diffusion. EUSFNA was performed. Ultrasonographic findings were coherent and aspiration was made for cytology, and the diagnosis was confirmed. The tumor board posed indication for surgery.

The surgeon performs a robotic spleen-preserving distal pancreatectomy, beginning with the exposure of the pancreas by entering the lesser sac and cutting vasobrevia. Bipolar coagulation is used to control small vessels, while low-power monopolar coagulation and scissors are employed to divide the omentum. Small clips secure the vasobrevia. The phrenocolic ligament is divided to mobilize the left colonic flexure, and the tail of the pancreas is dissected smoothly from the kidney along the avascular plane of the Gerota's fascia.

The surgeon dissects the main trunk of the splenic artery proximally to the lesion, passing a vessel loop under the artery for traction and control. Small vascular clamps are used during the delicate dissection. Once the artery is separated from the pancreas, a small trunk directed to the lower pole of the spleen is identified, ligated, and divided as it is immersed in the lesion.

The surgeon removes the vascular clamp as the dissection nears completion. The small polar artery is divided distally to the lesion, revealing the lower proximal branch of the splenic vein. The pancreatic tail is carefully divided, with attempts to identify the pancreatic duct, which is very thin at this level. Upon identification, the duct is directly ligated.

The surgeon progressively dissects the tail of the pancreas away from the splenic vein using blunt and sharp dissection techniques, along with bipolar coagulation. A venous branch is identified, ligated, and divided. The resection is completed without intraoperative bleeding, and a hemostatic and sealing patch is applied to the pancreatic stump. The specimen is retrieved in a bag through a small incision, and a small drain is placed in the left hypochondrium for twenty-four hours.

Postoperative course was uneventful, with early recovery and discharge. The anatomopathological examination confirmed the diagnosis of solid papillary epithelial neoplasia of the pancreas. The tumor resulted completely excised and all the ten retrieved lymph nodes were free.
